exercises.title: Store and Retrieve String Values with SET and GET
exercises.problemHtml:
The string is Redis's most basic value: a key maps to one string, and almost everything else builds on it. SET writes a key, GET reads it back, and reading a key that was never set returns the special value (nil) rather than an error — a distinction your code must handle.

Using redis-cli, model a small user profile spread across several keys:

  • Store "Ann", "ann@example.com", "Hanoi", and "member" under user:1:name, user:1:email, user:1:city, and user:1:role.
  • Read all four keys back.
  • Overwrite user:1:name with "Annabelle" — a plain SET replaces the existing value with no error — and read it again.
  • Read user:1:phone, a key you never set, and observe it returns (nil).

Use the colon-delimited key naming (user:1:name) that is the conventional way to namespace keys in Redis. The scaffold lists the commands with the values left blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. You issue redis-cli commands; the "input" is the commands themselves.
exercises.outputSpec: The four GETs return "Ann", "ann@example.com", "Hanoi", "member"; after overwrite GET user:1:name returns "Annabelle"; GET user:1:phone returns (nil).
exercises.constraints: Use only SET and GET. Do not create the phone key. Use the colon-namespaced key names exactly as given.

examplesJson.input: SET user:1:name "Ann"  then  GET user:1:name
examplesJson.explanation: SET replies OK; GET returns the stored string in quotes.

examplesJson.input: GET user:1:phone  (never set)
examplesJson.explanation: Reading a key that does not exist yields the nil reply — not an error and not an empty string.

hintsJson: Two commands do everything here: one writes, one reads.
SET key value writes; GET key reads it back.
A second SET on the same key simply replaces the old value and replies OK.
SET user:1:name "Annabelle" overwrites; GET user:1:phone on an unset key returns (nil).

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SET user:1:name ____
SET user:1:email ____
SET user:1:city ____
SET user:1:role ____
GET user:1:name
GET user:1:email
GET user:1:city
GET user:1:role
SET user:1:name ____
GET user:1:name
GET user:1:phone

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET user:1:name "Ann"
SET user:1:email "ann@example.com"
SET user:1:city "Hanoi"
SET user:1:role "member"
GET user:1:name
GET user:1:email
GET user:1:city
GET user:1:role
SET user:1:name "Annabelle"
GET user:1:name
GET user:1:phone

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Every SET is an unconditional write: it creates the key if absent and replaces the value if present, always replying OK. That is why SET user:1:name "Annabelle" silently overwrites "Ann" — Redis strings have no "already exists" protection unless you ask for it with the NX option. GET returns the current value as a quoted string in redis-cli.

The one subtlety worth internalising is the difference between a missing key and an empty value. GET user:1:phone returns (nil) because the key was never written; that is distinct from a key holding the empty string "". In client libraries this surfaces as null/None versus "", and treating them the same is a classic source of bugs — a cache "miss" (nil) must trigger a recompute, whereas a stored empty string is a real cached value. The colon namespacing (user:1:name) carries no special meaning to Redis itself; it is a widely-followed convention that keeps a flat keyspace organised and makes pattern-based tooling easier later. Storing a profile as several discrete keys, as here, is the simplest model; a Redis hash can hold all the fields under one key, which you will meet in the data-structures module.

exercises.title: Inspect and Remove Keys with EXISTS, TYPE, and DEL
exercises.problemHtml:
Beyond reading values, you often need to ask about a key itself: does it exist, what type of value does it hold, and how do I remove it. EXISTS, TYPE, and DEL are the foundational key-management commands, and their return values are counts and labels rather than the stored data.

Using redis-cli, perform this sequence:

  • Create three keys: session:abc = "active", session:xyz = "idle", session:pqr = "pending".
  • EXISTS session:abc returns 1; EXISTS session:abc session:xyz session:pqr counts all three and returns 3.
  • TYPE session:abc reports string.
  • DEL session:abc returns 1 (one key removed); EXISTS session:abc is now 0.
  • DEL session:abc again returns 0 — nothing to remove.
  • DEL session:xyz session:pqr deletes both remaining keys and returns 2.

Notice that EXISTS can count several keys at once, and that DEL is safe to repeat. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. You issue the key-management commands via redis-cli.
exercises.outputSpec: EXISTS returns 1, then 3 (three keys), then 0 after deletion; TYPE returns string; the first DEL of a key returns 1, a repeat returns 0, and a two-key DEL returns 2.
exercises.constraints: Use EXISTS, TYPE, and DEL. Do not use GET to check existence — use EXISTS.

examplesJson.input: SET session:abc/xyz/pqr; EXISTS session:abc session:xyz session:pqr
examplesJson.explanation: EXISTS with multiple keys returns how many of them exist — all three do, so 3.

examplesJson.input: DEL session:abc  then  DEL session:abc  again
examplesJson.explanation: The first DEL removes one key (returns 1); the repeat finds nothing and returns 0 — DEL is idempotent.

hintsJson: These commands answer questions about the key, so they return counts and labels, not the value.
EXISTS key returns 1 or 0; passing several keys returns how many exist. TYPE key returns the value type name.
DEL key returns how many keys it actually removed.
A second DEL on the same key returns 0 because there is nothing left to delete.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SET session:abc ____
SET session:xyz ____
SET session:pqr ____
EXISTS session:abc
EXISTS session:abc session:xyz session:pqr
TYPE session:abc
DEL session:abc
EXISTS session:abc
DEL session:abc
DEL session:xyz session:pqr

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET session:abc "active"
SET session:xyz "idle"
SET session:pqr "pending"
EXISTS session:abc
EXISTS session:abc session:xyz session:pqr
TYPE session:abc
DEL session:abc
EXISTS session:abc
DEL session:abc
DEL session:xyz session:pqr

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
These three commands report on keys rather than return their contents. EXISTS returns a count — 1 when a key is present, 0 when it is not, and when given several keys it returns how many of them exist (here 2). TYPE returns the value's data type as a bare word, string here, which later becomes essential when a key might hold a list, hash, or set. DEL returns the number of keys actually removed, which is why the first delete reports 1 and the repeat reports 0.

That count-based return is the practical lesson: DEL is idempotent, so cleanup code can call it without first checking existence and without fearing an error on an already-gone key — a property you rely on when tearing down sessions or cache entries that may or may not still be there. The common mistake is using GET key to test existence: it works for strings but returns (nil) for a missing key and would fail outright on a non-string type, whereas EXISTS answers the presence question uniformly for every value type. Prefer EXISTS for "is it there" and reserve GET for "what is its value". One production note: DEL frees the memory synchronously, which for a very large value can briefly block the server; UNLINK is the non-blocking alternative that reclaims memory in the background.

exercises.title: Maintain an Atomic Counter with INCR and INCRBY
exercises.problemHtml:
Counting events — page views, likes, retries — is one of Redis's most common jobs, and it must be correct even when many clients count at once. INCR solves this: it increments a key's integer value atomically, so two simultaneous increments never lose a count the way a read-modify-write in application code would. It also initialises a missing key to 0 before incrementing.

Using redis-cli on a fresh keyspace, work the key page:home:views:

  • INCR it twice — the missing key is treated as 0, so the results are 1 then 2.
  • INCRBY it by 10 → 12; DECR once → 11; DECRBY by 3 → 8.
  • GET the key and observe the value comes back as the string "8", not a bare integer.
  • Reset it with SET page:home:views 100, then INCR once more → 101, and GET → "101".

The point to absorb: the counter is stored as a string that Redis interprets numerically for the increment family, and a plain SET can reset it. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database; the key page:home:views does not exist initially.
exercises.outputSpec: INCR returns 1 then 2; INCRBY 10 -> 12; DECR -> 11; DECRBY 3 -> 8; GET -> "8"; after SET 100, INCR -> 101 and GET -> "101".
exercises.constraints: Use INCR, INCRBY, DECR, and DECRBY — do not compute the new value in application code. Do not pre-create the key before the first INCR.

examplesJson.input: INCR page:home:views  (key absent)  then INCR again
examplesJson.explanation: A missing key starts at 0, so the first INCR returns 1; the second returns 2.

examplesJson.input: SET page:home:views 100  then  INCR page:home:views  then  GET page:home:views
examplesJson.explanation: SET overwrites the counter to 100; INCR returns the integer 101; GET returns it as the string "101".

hintsJson: Never read the count into your app, add one, and write it back — that loses updates under concurrency. Let Redis do the add.
INCR key increments by one and returns the new value; a missing key is treated as 0 first.
INCRBY key 10 adds ten; DECR key subtracts one; DECRBY key 3 subtracts three.
After the operations, GET returns the number formatted as a string, e.g. "8"; a plain SET can reset the counter.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
INCR page:home:views
INCR page:home:views
INCRBY page:home:views ____
DECR page:home:views
DECRBY page:home:views ____
GET page:home:views
SET page:home:views 100
INCR page:home:views
GET page:home:views
DECRBY page:home:views 50
INCRBY page:home:views 9
GET page:home:views

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
INCR page:home:views
INCR page:home:views
INCRBY page:home:views 10
DECR page:home:views
DECRBY page:home:views 3
GET page:home:views
SET page:home:views 100
INCR page:home:views
GET page:home:views
DECRBY page:home:views 50
INCRBY page:home:views 9
GET page:home:views

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The whole value of INCR is atomicity. Redis executes each command to completion before starting the next, so an increment reads, adds, and writes as one indivisible step. That is why a counter under heavy concurrency stays correct with INCR but corrupts if you emulate it with GET then SET in your application: between your read and your write, another client can slip in, and one of the two increments is lost. Pushing the arithmetic into Redis removes that race entirely.

Two details reward attention. First, a non-existent key is treated as 0 before the operation, so you never need to initialise a counter — the first INCR returns 1. Second, the counter is physically stored as a string; the INCR family parses it as a 64-bit integer, increments, and writes it back as a string, which is why GET returns "8" in quotes and a plain SET page:home:views 100 can reset it. A consequence and common trap: if the key holds a value that is not a valid integer (say "12x" or a float), INCR fails with value is not an integer or out of range. For fractional counters use INCRBYFLOAT, and remember the increments overflow past the signed 64-bit range rather than growing without bound.

exercises.title: Expire Keys with TTL, EXPIRE, and PERSIST
exercises.problemHtml:
A key that expires on its own is what makes Redis a natural cache and session store: set a one-time password to live for five minutes and it deletes itself, no cleanup job required. You attach a time-to-live at write time with SET ... EX, inspect it with TTL, add or change it with EXPIRE, and remove it with PERSIST.

Using redis-cli, perform this sequence:

  • Write otp:123 = "999000" with a 300-second expiry using SET ... EX 300; TTL otp:123 reports about 300.
  • PERSIST otp:123 removes the expiry and returns 1; TTL otp:123 now returns -1 ("exists, no expiry").
  • TTL missing:key returns -2 ("key does not exist").
  • Write k="v" plainly, then EXPIRE k 60 (returns 1); TTL k is about 60.
  • Overwrite with a plain SET k "v2", then TTL k — it is now -1, because a plain SET clears the TTL.

Learn the two negative TTL codes, and the fact that overwriting a key drops its expiry. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Commands set, inspect, and remove expirations.
exercises.outputSpec: TTL returns ~300, then -1 after PERSIST; TTL of a missing key returns -2; EXPIRE returns 1 and TTL ~60; after a plain SET overwrite, TTL returns -1 (TTL cleared).
exercises.constraints: Use SET ... EX for the initial expiry and EXPIRE for the separate one. Observe that a plain SET clears an existing TTL (use KEEPTTL to preserve it).

examplesJson.input: SET otp:123 "999000" EX 300  then  TTL otp:123
examplesJson.explanation: The key is stored with a 300-second lifetime; TTL reports the seconds left (300 immediately after).

examplesJson.input: PERSIST otp:123; TTL otp:123; TTL missing:key
examplesJson.explanation: PERSIST removes the expiry (1). TTL then returns -1 (no expiry) for the live key and -2 for a key that does not exist.

examplesJson.input: SET k "v"; EXPIRE k 60; SET k "v2"; TTL k
examplesJson.explanation: EXPIRE sets a 60s TTL, but the plain SET k "v2" overwrite discards it, so TTL returns -1.

hintsJson: Expiry can be attached at write time or added later. Two different commands, one effect.
SET key value EX 300 writes with a 300-second TTL; TTL key reports the remaining seconds.
PERSIST key strips the expiry so the key lives forever; it returns 1 if it removed one.
Memorise the codes: TTL returns -1 for a key with no expiry and -2 for a key that is absent. A plain SET on the key clears any TTL unless you add KEEPTTL.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SET otp:123 "999000" EX ____
TTL otp:123
PERSIST otp:123
TTL otp:123
TTL missing:key
SET k "v"
EXPIRE k 60
TTL k
SET k "v2"
TTL k
SET k2 "temp"
EXPIRE k2 120
TTL k2
PERSIST k2
TTL k2
SET k3 "z" EX 45
TTL k3

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET otp:123 "999000" EX 300
TTL otp:123
PERSIST otp:123
TTL otp:123
TTL missing:key
SET k "v"
EXPIRE k 60
TTL k
SET k "v2"
TTL k
SET k2 "temp"
EXPIRE k2 120
TTL k2
PERSIST k2
TTL k2
SET k3 "z" EX 45
TTL k3

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Attaching a lifetime to a key is what turns Redis into a self-cleaning store. SET otp:123 "999000" EX 300 writes the value and a 300-second countdown in one atomic command; once it elapses, Redis deletes the key automatically, so an expiring one-time password needs no separate cleanup. TTL reports the seconds remaining, and PERSIST cancels the countdown, making the key permanent again (it returns 1 when it actually removed a TTL).

The detail most people get wrong is the two negative TTL results, because they look similar but mean opposite things. -1 means the key exists but has no expiration; -2 means the key does not exist at all. Code that treats both as "no data" will happily overwrite a live permanent key, so branch on them explicitly. The second trap is demonstrated directly by the last two commands: a plain SET on a key that already has a TTL clears that TTL, which is why TTL k ends at -1 after SET k "v2". This is a frequent cause of cache entries that mysteriously stop expiring after an update; pass KEEPTTL (SET k "v2" KEEPTTL) when you want the expiry preserved across a rewrite. Finally, prefer setting the TTL atomically with SET ... EX over a separate SET then EXPIRE: the two-command version leaves a brief window where the key exists with no expiry, and if the client crashes in between, the key leaks forever.

exercises.title: Set a Key Only If Absent with SET NX
exercises.problemHtml:
Sometimes a write must only happen if the key is not already taken — claiming a lock, reserving a unique username, or making an operation run exactly once. The NX ("only if it does Not eXist") option gives you this atomically: SET key value NX stores the value and returns OK only when the key was absent, and returns (nil) without changing anything when it was already present.

Using redis-cli, perform this sequence:

  • SETNX lock:job "worker1" claims the free key → 1; a second SETNX lock:job "worker2" finds it taken → 0; GET lock:job is still "worker1" (first writer won).
  • SET config:mode "prod" NX → OK; SET config:mode "dev" NX → (nil) (unchanged); GET config:mode is still "prod".
  • Release the first lock with DEL lock:job (→ 1), then re-claim it atomically with a safety timeout: SET lock:job "worker3" NX EX 30 → OK, and TTL lock:job is about 30.

Both SETNX (dedicated command) and SET ... NX (the modern option form) express the same rule; the option form also composes with EX. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Independent keys demonstrate the conditional write and a lock with a timeout.
exercises.outputSpec: SETNX returns 1 then 0; GET lock:job stays "worker1". SET ... NX returns OK then (nil); GET config:mode stays "prod". After DEL, SET ... NX EX 30 returns OK and TTL ~30.
exercises.constraints: Use SETNX and/or SET ... NX. The second write to each key must not change it. Do not use EXISTS-then-SET (not atomic). Re-claim the released lock with SET ... NX EX to add a timeout.

examplesJson.input: SETNX lock:job "worker1"  then  SETNX lock:job "worker2"
examplesJson.explanation: The first claim succeeds (1); the second finds the key taken and fails to set (0).

examplesJson.input: DEL lock:job; SET lock:job "worker3" NX EX 30; TTL lock:job
examplesJson.explanation: After release, the NX set succeeds and EX 30 gives the lock a 30-second self-expiry, so TTL reports ~30.

hintsJson: The rule is "write only if nobody else has". Checking with EXISTS first is a race; you need one atomic command.
SETNX key value sets only when the key is absent, returning 1 or 0.
The modern equivalent is SET key value NX, which returns OK or (nil) and can add EX for a timeout.
After DEL releases the lock, SET lock:job "worker3" NX EX 30 re-claims it with a 30-second safety expiry.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SETNX lock:job ____
SETNX lock:job ____
GET lock:job
SET config:mode "prod" NX
SET config:mode "dev" NX
GET config:mode
DEL lock:job
SET lock:job "worker3" NX EX 30
TTL lock:job
GET lock:job
DEL config:mode
SET config:mode "staging" NX
GET config:mode

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SETNX lock:job "worker1"
SETNX lock:job "worker2"
GET lock:job
SET config:mode "prod" NX
SET config:mode "dev" NX
GET config:mode
DEL lock:job
SET lock:job "worker3" NX EX 30
TTL lock:job
GET lock:job
DEL config:mode
SET config:mode "staging" NX
GET config:mode

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The NX semantics are "set only if the key does not already exist", executed atomically. That atomicity is the entire point. The tempting alternative — EXISTS key, and if it returns 0 then SET key value — has a gap between the check and the write in which another client can claim the key, so two workers both believe they won. SETNX/SET ... NX collapses check-and-set into one indivisible operation, so exactly one caller gets the 1/OK and every other gets 0/(nil). This is the primitive behind "first writer wins", one-time initialisation, and the simplest form of a distributed lock.

Note the two return conventions: the dedicated SETNX command returns an integer (1/0), while the option form SET ... NX returns OK/(nil). They are otherwise equivalent, and the modern option form is preferred because it composes with other options in one command — crucially SET lock:job "worker3" NX EX 30, which claims a lock and gives it a safety timeout atomically, as the last commands show. That combination matters: a lock taken with NX but no expiry becomes a permanent deadlock if the holder crashes before releasing it, whereas the EX ensures it self-heals. For fully-correct distributed locking you also need a unique token and a checked release (only delete if the token is still yours), but the atomic conditional set demonstrated here is the foundation everything else is built on.

exercises.title: Read and Write Many Keys at Once with MSET and MGET
exercises.problemHtml:
Issuing a hundred single GETs means a hundred network round trips. MSET and MGET collapse many reads or writes into one command and one round trip, which is often the single biggest latency win when working with Redis. MSETNX adds an all-or-nothing guarantee for batch writes.

Using redis-cli, perform this sequence:

  • MSET u:1 "Ann" u:2 "Bob" u:3 "Cy" writes three keys at once → OK.
  • MGET u:1 u:2 u:4 reads three keys in order; u:4 was never set, so its slot is (nil).
  • MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:1 "X" sets only if all keys are absent; because u:1 exists, none are written → 0; GET u:4 is still (nil).
  • MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:5 "Ed" — now both target keys are free, so it writes both → 1; MGET u:4 u:5 returns "Di" and "Ed".

The lesson is that MGET preserves position (missing keys become nil in place) and MSETNX is atomic across the whole batch. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Batch commands write and read several keys together.
exercises.outputSpec: MSET returns OK; MGET returns ["Ann","Bob",nil]; the first MSETNX returns 0 (u:1 exists) and u:4 stays nil; the second MSETNX returns 1 and MGET u:4 u:5 returns ["Di","Ed"].
exercises.constraints: Use MSET, MGET, and MSETNX. Do not fall back to individual SET/GET calls. Rely on MSETNX being all-or-nothing.

examplesJson.input: MSET u:1 "Ann" u:2 "Bob" u:3 "Cy"  then  MGET u:1 u:2 u:4
examplesJson.explanation: MGET returns one array slot per requested key in order; u:4 was never set so slot 3 is (nil).

examplesJson.input: MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:1 "X"  then  MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:5 "Ed"
examplesJson.explanation: The first fails because u:1 exists (all-or-nothing, so u:4 is not set either); the second succeeds because u:4 and u:5 are both free.

hintsJson: Batching many keys into one command removes network round trips — the usual bottleneck.
MSET k1 v1 k2 v2 ... writes them all; MGET k1 k2 ... reads them all in order.
A key missing in MGET does not shrink the result — its position holds (nil).
MSETNX writes only if every listed key is absent; one existing key makes the whole batch a no-op returning 0.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
MSET u:1 ____ u:2 ____ u:3 ____
MGET u:1 u:2 u:4
MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:1 "X"
GET u:4
MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:5 "Ed"
MGET u:4 u:5
MSET u:6 "Fi" u:7 "Gu"
MGET u:5 u:6 u:7 u:99
MSETNX u:8 "Hu" u:9 "Iv"
MGET u:6 u:7 u:8 u:9

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
MSET u:1 "Ann" u:2 "Bob" u:3 "Cy"
MGET u:1 u:2 u:4
MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:1 "X"
GET u:4
MSETNX u:4 "Di" u:5 "Ed"
MGET u:4 u:5
MSET u:6 "Fi" u:7 "Gu"
MGET u:5 u:6 u:7 u:99
MSETNX u:8 "Hu" u:9 "Iv"
MGET u:6 u:7 u:8 u:9

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
MSET and MGET exist for one reason: throughput. Network latency, not Redis's in-memory work, dominates the cost of many small operations, so replacing N round trips with one is frequently a 10x-or-more improvement for bulk reads and writes. MGET returns a positional array — one slot per key you asked for, in the same order — and a key that does not exist yields (nil) in its slot rather than being dropped. That positional guarantee is what lets you zip the results back onto your list of requested keys without ambiguity; assuming missing keys are omitted would misalign every value after the gap.

MSETNX adds atomic all-or-nothing semantics: it writes the whole batch only if every listed key is currently absent. The first call returns 0 and writes nothing because u:1 exists — so u:4 stays (nil) even though it was free — while the second call returns 1 because both u:4 and u:5 are absent. This makes MSETNX suitable for initialising a group of related keys exactly once. The main caveat to remember for later: in Redis Cluster, multi-key commands require all keys to live in the same hash slot, so bulk operations across arbitrary keys may need hash-tag key design or client-side grouping to work in a sharded deployment.

exercises.title: Manipulate String Fragments with APPEND, STRLEN, and GETRANGE
exercises.problemHtml:
Redis strings are more than opaque blobs — you can grow them and slice them server-side without transferring the whole value. APPEND adds to the end and returns the new length, STRLEN reports the length, GETRANGE extracts a substring by index, and SETRANGE overwrites part of it in place.

Using redis-cli, perform this sequence:

  • SET msg "hello", then APPEND msg " world" → the new total length 11; STRLEN msg → 11.
  • GETRANGE msg 0 4 → "hello" (indices 0..4 inclusive); GETRANGE msg -5 -1 → "world" (last five characters).
  • APPEND fresh "new" on a key that does not exist → 3 (APPEND creates it); GET fresh → "new".
  • SETRANGE msg 0 "H" overwrites index 0 → returns the length 11; GET msg → "Hello world".

Note that GETRANGE indices are inclusive on both ends, negative indices count from the end, and APPEND on a missing key creates it. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Commands build, slice, and edit string keys.
exercises.outputSpec: APPEND returns 11; STRLEN 11; GETRANGE 0 4 "hello"; GETRANGE -5 -1 "world"; APPEND on a missing key returns 3; SETRANGE returns 11 and GET msg is "Hello world".
exercises.constraints: Use APPEND, STRLEN, GETRANGE, and SETRANGE. GETRANGE indices are inclusive; use negative indices for the tail slice.

examplesJson.input: SET msg "hello"  then  APPEND msg " world"  then  STRLEN msg
examplesJson.explanation: "hello" is 5 chars; appending " world" (6 chars) makes 11, which APPEND returns and STRLEN confirms.

examplesJson.input: GETRANGE msg -5 -1  then  SETRANGE msg 0 "H"  then  GET msg
examplesJson.explanation: Negative indices give the last 5 chars; SETRANGE replaces index 0 with H and returns the length; GET shows the edited string.

hintsJson: You can change and read parts of a string without sending the whole value over the wire.
APPEND key text adds to the end and returns the resulting length; STRLEN key returns the length.
GETRANGE key start end extracts a substring; both indices are inclusive and negatives count from the end.
SETRANGE key offset text overwrites in place starting at offset and returns the new length.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SET msg "hello"
APPEND msg ____
STRLEN msg
GETRANGE msg 0 4
GETRANGE msg -5 -1
APPEND fresh "new"
GET fresh
SETRANGE msg 0 "H"
GET msg
STRLEN msg
GETRANGE msg 6 10
APPEND fresh "-tag"
GET fresh
STRLEN fresh

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET msg "hello"
APPEND msg " world"
STRLEN msg
GETRANGE msg 0 4
GETRANGE msg -5 -1
APPEND fresh "new"
GET fresh
SETRANGE msg 0 "H"
GET msg
STRLEN msg
GETRANGE msg 6 10
APPEND fresh "-tag"
GET fresh
STRLEN fresh

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
These commands operate on the string in place on the server, which saves bandwidth and round trips. APPEND concatenates onto the existing value — or creates the key if absent, as with fresh — and conveniently returns the new length, so you can track size as you build. STRLEN reports that length independently. GETRANGE slices out a substring by byte index, and the indexing rules are the crux: both start and end are inclusive, so 0 4 returns five characters, and negative indices count from the end with -1 being the last character, making -5 -1 the trailing five. SETRANGE overwrites bytes starting at an offset and returns the resulting length.

The off-by-one trap is expecting GETRANGE key 0 5 to return five characters as it would in a language with exclusive end bounds; here it returns six (indices 0,1,2,3,4,5). Because Redis strings are binary-safe — they can hold arbitrary bytes, not just text — these operations count bytes rather than Unicode code points, so slicing multibyte UTF-8 by character position can split a character; index by byte offsets you control. APPEND runs in amortised constant time thanks to Redis over-allocating string capacity, so incrementally building a value is efficient rather than quadratic, though for structured accumulation a list or stream is usually the better data type.

exercises.title: Rename Keys Safely with RENAME and RENAMENX
exercises.problemHtml:
Promoting a draft to published, or swapping a freshly-built value into a live key, is a rename. RENAME moves a key's value to a new name atomically, but it will happily clobber an existing destination — so when that is unsafe, RENAMENX renames only if the destination is free. COPY duplicates a key without removing the source.

Using redis-cli, perform this sequence:

  • SET draft:1 "final content", then RENAME draft:1 published:1 → OK; GET published:1 → "final content"; EXISTS draft:1 → 0 (the source name is gone).
  • SET a "x", SET b "y", then RENAMENX a b → 0 — the destination b exists, so the rename is refused.
  • COPY a c → 1; GET c → "x" and a still holds "x" (COPY preserves the source).
  • RENAMENX a d → 1 — the destination d is free, so this rename succeeds.

The contrast to internalise: RENAME overwrites, RENAMENX protects, COPY preserves the original. The scaffold lists the commands.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Commands rename and copy keys, including a blocked rename and a successful guarded one.
exercises.outputSpec: RENAME returns OK (source EXISTS becomes 0); RENAMENX onto an existing key returns 0; COPY returns 1 leaving the source intact; RENAMENX onto a free key returns 1.
exercises.constraints: Use RENAME, RENAMENX, and COPY. Do not emulate rename with GET + SET + DEL (not atomic). Expect RENAMENX to refuse an occupied destination and accept a free one.

examplesJson.input: SET draft:1 "final content"; RENAME draft:1 published:1; EXISTS draft:1
examplesJson.explanation: RENAME moves the value to published:1 and removes the old name, so EXISTS draft:1 is 0.

examplesJson.input: SET a "x"; SET b "y"; RENAMENX a b; COPY a c; RENAMENX a d
examplesJson.explanation: RENAMENX a b is refused because b exists (0); COPY a c duplicates to the free key c (1); RENAMENX a d succeeds because d is free (1).

hintsJson: Renaming should be one atomic step, not a read, write, and delete in your app.
RENAME old new moves the value and removes the old name, returning OK.
RENAMENX old new does the same only if new is free, returning 1 or 0.
COPY src dst duplicates without deleting src, returning 1 when it copied.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SET draft:1 ____
RENAME draft:1 published:1
GET published:1
EXISTS draft:1
SET a "x"
SET b "y"
RENAMENX a b
COPY a c
GET c
RENAMENX a d
GET d
EXISTS a
COPY d e
GET e
EXISTS c d e
COPY d f
GET f
EXISTS c d e f

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET draft:1 "final content"
RENAME draft:1 published:1
GET published:1
EXISTS draft:1
SET a "x"
SET b "y"
RENAMENX a b
COPY a c
GET c
RENAMENX a d
GET d
EXISTS a
COPY d e
GET e
EXISTS c d e
COPY d f
GET f
EXISTS c d e f

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
All three commands change the keyspace atomically, which is why you reach for them instead of stitching together GET, SET, and DEL in application code — that manual sequence has windows where the value exists under both names, neither name, or is half-written. RENAME old new atomically moves the value to the new name and deletes the old one, so afterwards EXISTS draft:1 is 0. The catch is that RENAME overwrites the destination without warning if it already holds a value, which can silently destroy data.

RENAMENX is the guarded version: it performs the rename only when the destination does not exist, returning 1 on success and 0 when it refused — that is the 0 when renaming a onto the existing b, and the 1 when renaming a onto the free d. COPY src dst is different again: it duplicates the value to a new key and leaves the source in place, returning 1 when it copied (it refuses an existing destination unless you add the REPLACE option). One sharp edge to remember: RENAME on a source key that does not exist raises an error (no such key), unlike the forgiving DEL, so guard renames whose source might be absent. Renaming a key preserves its TTL, which is what makes the "build under a temp name, then rename into place" cache-swap pattern work cleanly.

exercises.title: Build a Fixed-Window Rate Limiter with INCR and EXPIRE
exercises.problemHtml:
The canonical first "real" Redis pattern is a fixed-window rate limiter: allow at most N actions per time window per user. It is built from two primitives you already know — an atomic counter (INCR) and an expiry (EXPIRE) — combined so the counter resets itself when the window ends.

The rule: each request runs INCR on a key like rate:user:1. If the result is 1, this is the first request of a new window, so set the window length with EXPIRE ... 60. If the returned count exceeds the limit, the request is rejected. Simulate a limit of 5 per 60 seconds with six requests:

  • Request 1: INCR rate:user:1 → 1; because it is 1, EXPIRE rate:user:1 60 (→ 1).
  • Requests 2–5: INCR returns 2, 3, 4, 5 — all at or under the limit of 5, so allowed. Do not reset the TTL.
  • TTL rate:user:1 stays around 60, proving the window counts down from the first request.
  • Request 6: INCR returns 6 — this exceeds 5, so the request is rejected by your application logic.

The scaffold lays out the request sequence. Focus on why EXPIRE is applied only when the counter is 1, and how the returned count drives the allow/reject decision.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database; rate:user:1 does not exist. Simulate six requests within one window, limit 5.
exercises.outputSpec: INCR returns 1..6 across the six requests; EXPIRE (only after the first) returns 1; TTL stays ~60; the count of 6 signals rejection because it exceeds the limit of 5.
exercises.constraints: Apply EXPIRE only when INCR returns 1 (start of window). Do not re-set the TTL on later requests, or the window would never end (a sliding bug). Do not store timestamps. A returned count greater than 5 means reject.

examplesJson.input: First request: INCR rate:user:1 (absent key) then EXPIRE rate:user:1 60
examplesJson.explanation: The counter starts at 1 for a new window; EXPIRE sets the 60-second window and returns 1.

examplesJson.input: The 5th and 6th requests: INCR rate:user:1 (twice)
examplesJson.explanation: Count 5 is at the limit (allowed); count 6 exceeds the limit of 5, so the application rejects that request.

hintsJson: You need a per-user counter that empties itself when the window ends — a counter plus an expiry.
On every request INCR the user key; the returned count is how many requests are in the current window.
Set the window exactly once: when INCR returns 1, the window just started, so EXPIRE it for the window length.
Never EXPIRE on later requests — that would keep pushing the reset forward so the window never closes. Reject when the returned count exceeds the limit.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
INCR rate:user:1
EXPIRE rate:user:1 ____
INCR rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
TTL rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
GET rate:user:1
TTL rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
GET rate:user:1
TTL rate:user:1

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
INCR rate:user:1
EXPIRE rate:user:1 60
INCR rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
TTL rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
GET rate:user:1
TTL rate:user:1
INCR rate:user:1
GET rate:user:1
TTL rate:user:1

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The pattern composes two atomic primitives into a self-resetting quota. Every request does INCR rate:user:1; the value it returns is the number of requests seen so far in the current window, so the caller simply compares it to the limit (5) and rejects when it goes over — which is exactly what the final count of 6 signals. The window itself is created exactly once: the very first request makes INCR return 1, and that return value of 1 is the signal to run EXPIRE rate:user:1 60. When the 60 seconds elapse, Redis deletes the key, and the next INCR starts a fresh window back at 1 — no cleanup job, no stored timestamps.

The critical rule, and the bug this exercise is designed to prevent, is applying EXPIRE only when the count is 1. If you reset the TTL on every request, the window's deadline keeps sliding forward and, for a steadily active user, the key never expires — the limit effectively becomes permanent and the window never resets. That is why the middle requests here leave the TTL untouched at ~60. Two production caveats: first, the INCR and the conditional EXPIRE are two separate commands, so a crash between them could leave a counter with no expiry; wrapping them in a Lua script (or MULTI/EXEC) makes the pair atomic. Second, a fixed window allows bursts at the boundary — up to 2N requests across two adjacent windows — which is why sliding-window or token-bucket variants exist; the fixed window is the simplest correct starting point and the foundation for those refinements.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Request arrives] --> B[INCR rate key]
  B --> C{Count equals 1}
  C -- yes --> D[EXPIRE key for window length]
  C -- no --> E[Leave TTL untouched]
  D --> F{Count over limit}
  E --> F
  F -- no --> G[Allow request]
  F -- yes --> H[Reject request]

exercises.title: Implement Cache-Aside with a Stampede Lock
exercises.problemHtml:
The most common way Redis is used is as a cache in front of a slow data source: check Redis first, and only compute from the database on a miss. The dangerous edge case is the cache stampede — when a hot key expires, hundreds of requests miss simultaneously and all hammer the database to rebuild the same value. A short-lived build lock lets only one request rebuild while the others wait or serve stale data.

Simulate the full cache-aside flow with two competing workers, in order:

  • GET cache:homepage → (nil): a cache miss.
  • Worker A claims the build lock: SET lock:homepage "A" NX EX 10 → OK. Worker B tries the same: SET lock:homepage "B" NX EX 10 → (nil), locked out.
  • Worker A stores the rebuilt value with a cache TTL: SET cache:homepage "rendered-html" EX 300 → OK; GET cache:homepage is now a hit → "rendered-html".
  • Worker A releases the lock: DEL lock:homepage → 1; TTL cache:homepage is about 300.
  • After release, a later worker C can re-acquire: SET lock:homepage "C" NX EX 10 → OK; GET cache:homepage still serves the cached "rendered-html".

The scaffold lays out the sequence. Focus on why the lock uses NX (only one winner) and EX (self-healing if the holder crashes).
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database; neither cache:homepage nor lock:homepage exists. Workers A and B contend, then worker C re-acquires after release.
exercises.outputSpec: The initial GET returns (nil); A wins the lock (OK) and B is refused (nil); storing returns OK; the next GET returns "rendered-html"; DEL returns 1; TTL ~300; after release C acquires (OK) and the cache still serves "rendered-html".
exercises.constraints: The build lock must use SET ... NX EX (both options). Only the lock winner writes the cache. Give the cached value its own TTL, separate from the lock TTL. Release the lock with DEL.

examplesJson.input: GET cache:homepage (cold); A: SET lock:homepage "A" NX EX 10; B: SET lock:homepage "B" NX EX 10
examplesJson.explanation: The cache is empty (nil). A wins the lock (OK); B is refused (nil) because NX allows only the first setter.

examplesJson.input: DEL lock:homepage; SET lock:homepage "C" NX EX 10; GET cache:homepage
examplesJson.explanation: Releasing the lock (1) lets worker C acquire it (OK); the cache still serves the stored value.

hintsJson: On a miss, you do not want every worker rebuilding at once. Let exactly one acquire a build lock.
The lock is just a key set with NX (only one winner) and EX (auto-expires if the holder dies): SET lock:key worker NX EX 10.
Only the worker that got OK computes and writes the cache; the others (nil) back off.
Store the rebuilt value with its own TTL (SET cache:key value EX 300), then release the lock with DEL so the next rebuild can proceed.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
GET cache:homepage
SET lock:homepage "A" NX EX 10
SET lock:homepage "B" NX EX 10
SET cache:homepage ____ EX 300
GET cache:homepage
DEL lock:homepage
TTL cache:homepage
SET lock:homepage "C" NX EX 10
GET cache:homepage

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
GET cache:homepage
SET lock:homepage "A" NX EX 10
SET lock:homepage "B" NX EX 10
SET cache:homepage "rendered-html" EX 300
GET cache:homepage
DEL lock:homepage
TTL cache:homepage
SET lock:homepage "C" NX EX 10
GET cache:homepage

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Cache-aside is the read path: look in Redis, and on a (nil) miss, compute the value from the source of truth and write it back with a TTL so future reads are hits. The problem this exercise targets appears only under load: when a popular key expires, many workers miss at the same instant and all rebuild it against the database — a stampede that can overwhelm the very system the cache was protecting. The fix is a build lock keyed on the thing being rebuilt. SET lock:homepage "A" NX EX 10 does two jobs in one atomic command: NX ensures only the first worker gets OK (every other gets (nil) and stands down), and EX 10 guarantees the lock disappears on its own even if the winning worker crashes mid-rebuild, so the system cannot deadlock.

Only the lock winner computes and writes SET cache:homepage "rendered-html" EX 300; note the cache's TTL (300) is deliberately separate from the lock's TTL (10), because they answer different questions — how long the value stays fresh versus how long a single rebuild may take. After writing, the winner releases the lock with DEL so the next rebuild after the cache eventually expires can proceed promptly, which is what lets worker C acquire the lock afterwards. Two refinements matter in production and are worth stating even though they are beyond this simulation: releasing the lock should be a checked delete (only delete if the stored token is still yours, via a small Lua script) so a worker whose lock already expired cannot delete a newer holder's lock; and the workers that lost the lock typically serve slightly stale data or retry after a brief sleep rather than blocking. The atomic SET ... NX EX lock is the load-bearing idea, and it is the same primitive that underlies the well-known Redis distributed-lock recipes.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant A as Worker A
  participant R as Redis
  participant B as Worker B
  A->>R: GET cache miss
  A->>R: SET lock NX EX 10 returns OK
  B->>R: SET lock NX EX 10 returns nil
  A->>R: SET cache value EX 300
  A->>R: DEL lock
  B->>R: GET cache now a hit
